1	= Resequence EIP
2	:doctitle: Resequence
3	:shortname: resequence
4	:description: Resequences (re-order) messages based on an expression
5	:since: 
6	:supportlevel: Stable
7	:tabs-sync-option:
8	
9	Camel supports the
10	http://www.enterpriseintegrationpatterns.com/Resequencer.html[Resequencer]
11	from the xref:enterprise-integration-patterns.adoc[EIP patterns].
12	
13	How can we get a stream of related but out-of-sequence messages back into the correct order?
14	
15	image::eip/Resequencer.gif[image]
16	
17	Use a stateful filter, a Resequencer, to collect and re-order messages so that they can be published to the output channel in a specified order.
18	
19	The Resequencer implementation in Camel uses an xref:manual::expression.adoc[Expression]
20	as the `Comparator` to re-order the messages.
21	By using the expression, then the messages
22	can easily be re-ordered by a message header or another piece of the message.
23	
24	Camel supports two re-sequencing algorithms:
25	
26	* xref:batchConfig-eip.adoc[Batch Resequencing] - *Default mode*: collects messages into a batch, sorts the messages and sends them to their output.
27	* xref:streamConfig-eip.adoc[Stream Resequencing] - re-orders (continuous) message streams based on the detection of gaps between messages.
28	
29	By default, the Resequencer does not support duplicate messages and will only keep the last message, in case a message arrives with the same message expression.
30	However, in the batch mode you can enable it to allow duplicates.
31	
32	== Options
33	
34	// eip options: START
35	include::partial$eip-options.adoc[]
36	// eip options: END
37	
38	== Exchange properties
39	
40	// eip exchangeProperties: START
41	include::partial$eip-exchangeProperties.adoc[]
42	// eip exchangeProperties: END
43	
44	== Batch Resequencing
45	
46	The following example shows how to use the Resequencer in xref:batchConfig-eip.adoc[batch mode] (default),
47	so that messages are sorted in order of the message body.
48	
49	That is messages are collected into a batch (either by a maximum number of messages per batch or using a timeout)
50	then they are sorted, and sent out, to continue being routed.
51	
52	In the example below, we re-order the message based on the content of the message body.
53	The default batch modes will collect up to 100 messages per batch, or timeout every second.
54	
55	[tabs]
56	====
57	Java::
58	+
59	[source,java]
60	----
61	from("direct:start")
62	    .resequence().body()
63	    .to("mock:result");
64	----
65	+
66	This is equivalent to:
67	+
68	[source,java]
69	----
70	from("direct:start")
71	    .resequence(body()).batch()
72	    .to("mock:result");
73	----
74	
75	XML::
76	+
77	[source,xml]
78	----
79	<route>
80	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
81	    <resequence>
82	        <simple>${body}</simple>
83	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
84	    </resequence>
85	</route>
86	----
87	
88	YAML::
89	+
90	[source,yaml]
91	----
92	- route:
93	    from:
94	      uri: direct:start
95	      steps:
96	        - resequence:
97	            expression:
98	              simple:
99	                expression: "${body}"
100	            steps:
101	              - to:
102	                  uri: mock:result
103	----
104	====
105	
106	The batch resequencer can be further configured via the `size()` and `timeout()` methods:
107	
108	[tabs]
109	====
110	Java::
111	+
112	[source,java]
113	----
114	from("direct:start")
115	    .resequence(body()).batch().size(300).timeout(4000L)
116	    .to("mock:result")
117	----
118	
119	XML::
120	+
121	[source,xml]
122	----
123	<route>
124	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
125	    <resequence>
126	        <batchConfig batchSize="300" batchTimeout="4000"/>
127	        <simple>${body}</simple>
128	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
129	    </resequence>
130	</route>
131	----
132	
133	YAML::
134	+
135	[source,yaml]
136	----
137	- route:
138	    from:
139	      uri: direct:start
140	      steps:
141	        - resequence:
142	            expression:
143	              simple:
144	                expression: "${body}"
145	            batchConfig:
146	              batchSize: 300
147	              batchTimeout: 4000
148	            steps:
149	              - to:
150	                  uri: mock:result
151	----
152	====
153	
154	This sets the batch size to 300 and the batch timeout to 4000 ms (by default,
155	the batch size is 100, and the timeout is 1000 ms).
156	
157	So the above example will reorder messages  in order of their bodies.
158	Typically, you'd use a header rather than the body to order things; or maybe a part of the body.
159	So you could replace this expression with:
160	
161	[tabs]
162	====
163	Java::
164	+
165	[source,java]
166	----
167	from("direct:start")
168	    .resequence(header("mySeqNo"))
169	    .to("mock:result")
170	----
171	
172	XML::
173	+
174	[source,xml]
175	----
176	<route>
177	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
178	    <resequence>
179	        <header>mySeqNo</header>
180	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
181	    </resequence>
182	</route>
183	----
184	
185	YAML::
186	+
187	[source,yaml]
188	----
189	- route:
190	    from:
191	      uri: direct:start
192	      steps:
193	        - resequence:
194	            expression:
195	              header:
196	                expression: mySeqNo
197	            steps:
198	              - to:
199	                  uri: mock:result
200	----
201	====
202	
203	This reorders messages using a custom sequence number with the header name mySeqNo.
204	
205	=== Allow Duplicates
206	
207	When allowing duplicates, then the resequencer retains the duplicate message instead
208	of keeping only the last duplicated message.
209	
210	In batch mode, you can turn on duplicates as follows:
211	
212	[tabs]
213	====
214	Java::
215	+
216	[source,java]
217	----
218	from("direct:start")
219	    .resequence(header("mySeqNo")).allowDuplicates()
220	    .to("mock:result")
221	----
222	
223	XML::
224	+
225	[source,xml]
226	----
227	<route>
228	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
229	    <resequence>
230	        <batchConfig allowDuplicates="true"/>
231	        <header>mySeqNo</header>
232	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
233	    </resequence>
234	</route>
235	----
236	
237	YAML::
238	+
239	[source,yaml]
240	----
241	- route:
242	    from:
243	      uri: direct:start
244	      steps:
245	        - resequence:
246	            expression:
247	              header:
248	                expression: mySeqNo
249	            batchConfig:
250	              allowDuplicates: "true"
251	            steps:
252	              - to:
253	                  uri: mock:result
254	----
255	====
256	
257	=== Reverse Ordering
258	
259	You can reverse the expression ordering.
260	By default, the order is based on `0..9,A..Z`,
261	which would let messages with low numbers be ordered first, and thus also outgoing first.
262	In some cases, you want to reverse the ordering.
263	
264	In batch mode, you can turn on reverse as follows:
265	
266	[tabs]
267	====
268	Java::
269	+
270	[source,java]
271	----
272	from("direct:start")
273	    .resequence(header("mySeqNo")).reverse()
274	    .to("mock:result")
275	----
276	
277	XML::
278	+
279	[source,xml]
280	----
281	<route>
282	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
283	    <resequence>
284	        <batchConfig reverse="true"/>
285	        <header>mySeqNo</header>
286	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
287	    </resequence>
288	</route>
289	----
290	
291	YAML::
292	+
293	[source,yaml]
294	----
295	- route:
296	    from:
297	      uri: direct:start
298	      steps:
299	        - resequence:
300	            expression:
301	              header:
302	                expression: mySeqNo
303	            batchConfig:
304	              reverse: "true"
305	            steps:
306	              - to:
307	                  uri: mock:result
308	----
309	====
310	
311	=== Ignoring invalid messages
312	
313	The Resequencer throws a `CamelExchangeException` if the incoming Exchange is not valid for the resequencer
314	such as, the expression cannot be evaluated due to a missing header.
315	
316	You can ignore these kinds of errors, and let the Resequencer skip the invalid Exchange.
317	
318	To do this, you do as follows:
319	
320	[tabs]
321	====
322	Java::
323	+
324	[source,java]
325	----
326	from("direct:start")
327	    .resequence(header("seqno")).batch()
328	        // ignore invalid exchanges (they are discarded)
329	        .ignoreInvalidExchanges()
330	    .to("mock:result");
331	----
332	
333	XML::
334	+
335	[source,xml]
336	----
337	<route>
338	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
339	    <resequence>
340	        <batchConfig ignoreInvalidExchanges="true"/>
341	        <header>seqno</header>
342	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
343	    </resequence>
344	</route>
345	----
346	
347	YAML::
348	+
349	[source,yaml]
350	----
351	- route:
352	    from:
353	      uri: direct:start
354	      steps:
355	        - resequence:
356	            expression:
357	              header:
358	                expression: seqno
359	            batchConfig:
360	              ignoreInvalidExchanges: "true"
361	            steps:
362	              - to:
363	                  uri: mock:result
364	----
365	====
366	
367	This option is available for both batch and stream mode.
368	
369	=== Resequence JMS messages based on JMSPriority
370	
371	It's now much easier to use the Resequencer to resequence messages from JMS queues based on JMSPriority.
372	For that to work, you need to use the two new options `allowDuplicates` and `reverse`.
373	
374	Notice this is *only* possible in the `batch` mode of the Resequencer.
375	
376	[tabs]
377	====
378	
379	Java::
380	+
381	[source,java]
382	----
383	from("jms:queue:foo")
384	    // sort by JMSPriority by allowing duplicates (the message can have the same JMSPriority)
385	    // and use reverse ordering so 9 is the first output (most important), and 0 is the last
386	    // use batch mode and fire every 3rd second
387	    .resequence(header("JMSPriority")).batch().timeout(3000).allowDuplicates().reverse()
388	    .to("mock:result");
389	----
390	
391	XML::
392	+
393	[source,xml]
394	----
395	<route>
396	    <from uri="jms:queue:foo"/>
397	    <resequence>
398	        <header>JMSPriority</header>
399	        <batchConfig reverse="true" allowDuplicates="true" batchTimeout="3000"/>
400	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
401	    </resequence>
402	</route>
403	----
404	
405	YAML::
406	+
407	[source,yaml]
408	----
409	- route:
410	    from:
411	      uri: jms:queue:foo
412	      steps:
413	        - resequence:
414	            expression:
415	              header:
416	                expression: JMSPriority
417	            batchConfig:
418	              reverse: "true"
419	              allowDuplicates: "true"
420	              batchTimeout: 3000
421	            steps:
422	              - to:
423	                  uri: mock:result
424	----
425	====
426	
427	== Stream Resequencing
428	
429	In streaming mode, then the Resequencer will send out messages as soon as possible when
430	a message with the next expected sequence number arrived.
431	
432	The streaming mode requires the messages to be re-ordered based on integer numeric values
433	that are ordered 1,2,3...N.
434	
435	The following example uses the header seqnum for the ordering:
436	
437	[tabs]
438	====
439	Java::
440	+
441	[source,java]
442	----
443	from("direct:start")
444	  .resequence(header("seqnum")).stream()
445	  .to("mock:result");
446	----
447	
448	XML::
449	+
450	[source,xml]
451	----
452	<route>
453	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
454	    <resequence>
455	        <streamConfig/>
456	        <header>seqno</header>
457	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
458	    </resequence>
459	</route>
460	----
461	
462	YAML::
463	+
464	[source,yaml]
465	----
466	- route:
467	    from:
468	      uri: direct:start
469	      steps:
470	        - resequence:
471	            expression:
472	              header:
473	                expression: seqnum
474	            streamConfig: {}
475	            steps:
476	              - to:
477	                  uri: mock:result
478	----
479	====
480	
481	The Resequencer keeps a backlog of pending messages in a backlog.
482	The default capacity is 1000 elements, which can be configured:
483	
484	[tabs]
485	====
486	Java::
487	+
488	[source,java]
489	----
490	from("direct:start")
491	    .resequence(header("seqnum")).stream().capacity(5000).timeout(4000)
492	    .to("mock:result")
493	----
494	
495	XML::
496	+
497	[source,xml]
498	----
499	<route>
500	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
501	    <resequence>
502	        <streamConfig capacity="5000" timeout="4000"/>
503	        <header>seqno</header>
504	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
505	    </resequence>
506	</route>
507	----
508	
509	YAML::
510	+
511	[source,yaml]
512	----
513	- route:
514	    from:
515	      uri: direct:start
516	      steps:
517	        - resequence:
518	            expression:
519	              header:
520	                expression: seqno
521	            streamConfig:
522	              timeout: 4000
523	              capacity: 5000
524	            steps:
525	              - to:
526	                  uri: mock:result
527	----
528	====
529	
530	This uses a capacity of 5000 elements. And the timeout has been set to 4 seconds.
531	In case of a timeout, then the resequencer disregards the current expected sequence number,
532	and moves to the next expected number.
533	
534	=== How streaming mode works?
535	
536	The stream-processing resequencer algorithm is based on the detection of gaps in a message stream rather than on a fixed batch size.
537	Gap detection in combination with timeouts removes the constraint of having to know the number of messages of a sequence (i.e.,
538	the batch size) in advance.
539	Messages must contain a unique sequence number for which a predecessor and a successor are known.
540	
541	For example,
542	a message with the sequence number 3 has a predecessor message with the sequence number 2 and a successor message with the sequence number 4.
543	The message sequence 2,3,5 has a gap because the successor of 3 is missing.
544	The resequencer therefore has to retain message 5 until message 4 arrives (or a timeout occurs).
545	
546	If the maximum time difference between messages (with successor/predecessor relationship with respect to the sequence number)
547	in a message stream is known, then the Resequencer timeout parameter should be set to this value.
548	
549	In this case, it is guaranteed that all messages of a stream are delivered in correct order to the next processor.
550	The lower the timeout value is compared to the out-of-sequence time difference,
551	the higher is the probability for out-of-sequence messages delivered by this Resequencer.
552	Large timeout values should be supported by sufficiently high capacity values.
553	The capacity parameter is used to prevent the Resequencer from running out of memory.
554	
555	=== Using custom streaming mode sequence expression
556	
557	By default, the stream Resequencer expects long sequence numbers,
558	but other sequence numbers types can be supported as well by providing a custom expression.
559	
560	[source,java]
561	----
562	public class MyFileNameExpression implements Expression {
563	
564	    public String getFileName(Exchange exchange) {
565	        return exchange.getIn().getBody(String.class);
566	    }
567	
568	    public Object evaluate(Exchange exchange) {
569	        // parser the file name with YYYYMMDD-DNNN pattern
570	        String fileName = getFileName(exchange);
571	        String[] files = fileName.split("-D");
572	        Long answer = Long.parseLong(files[0]) * 1000 + Long.parseLong(files[1]);
573	        return answer;
574	    }
575	
576	    public <T> T evaluate(Exchange exchange, Class<T> type) {
577	        Object result = evaluate(exchange);
578	        return exchange.getContext().getTypeConverter().convertTo(type, result);
579	    }
580	}
581	----
582	
583	And then you can use this expression in a Camel route:
584	
585	[tabs]
586	====
587	Java::
588	+
589	[source,java]
590	----
591	from("direct:start")
592	    .resequence(new MyFileNameExpression()).stream().timeout(2000)
593	    .to("mock:result");
594	----
595	
596	XML::
597	+
598	[source,xml]
599	----
600	<route>
601	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
602	    <resequence>
603	        <method beanType="com.foo.MyFileNameExpression"/>
604	        <streamConfig timeout="2000"/>
605	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
606	    </resequence>
607	</route>
608	----
609	
610	YAML::
611	+
612	[source,yaml]
613	----
614	- route:
615	    from:
616	      uri: direct:start
617	      steps:
618	        - resequence:
619	            expression:
620	              method:
621	                beanType: com.foo.MyFileNameExpression
622	            streamConfig:
623	              timeout: 2000
624	            steps:
625	              - to:
626	                  uri: mock:result
627	----
628	====
629	
630	
631	=== Rejecting old messages
632	
633	Rejecting old messages is used to prevent out of order messages from being sent,
634	regardless of the event that delivered messages downstream (capacity, timeout, etc).
635	
636	If enabled, the Resequencer will throw a `MessageRejectedException`
637	when an incoming Exchange is _older_ (based on the `Comparator`) than the last message delivered.
638	
639	This provides an extra level of control in regard to delayed message ordering.
640	
641	In the example below, old messages are rejected:
642	
643	[tabs]
644	====
645	Java::
646	+
647	[source,java]
648	----
649	from("direct:start")
650	    .resequence(header("seqno")).stream().timeout(1000).rejectOld()
651	    .to("mock:result");
652	----
653	
654	XML::
655	+
656	[source,xml]
657	----
658	<route>
659	    <from uri="direct:start"/>
660	    <resequence>
661	        <streamConfig rejectOld="true" timeout="1000"/>
662	        <header>seqno</header>
663	        <to uri="mock:result"/>
664	    </resequence>
665	</route>
666	----
667	
668	YAML::
669	+
670	[source,yaml]
671	----
672	- route:
673	    from:
674	      uri: direct:start
675	      steps:
676	        - resequence:
677	            expression:
678	              header:
679	                expression: seqno
680	            streamConfig:
681	              rejectOld: "true"
682	            steps:
683	              - to:
684	                  uri: mock:result
685	----
686	====
687	
688	If an old message is detected then Camel throws `MessageRejectedException` exception.
689

file.content:
= Resequence EIP
:doctitle: Resequence
:shortname: resequence
:description: Resequences (re-order) messages based on an expression
:since: 
:supportlevel: Stable
:tabs-sync-option:

Camel supports the
http://www.enterpriseintegrationpatterns.com/Resequencer.html[Resequencer]
from the xref:enterprise-integration-patterns.adoc[EIP patterns].

How can we get a stream of related but out-of-sequence messages back into the correct order?

image::eip/Resequencer.gif[image]

Use a stateful filter, a Resequencer, to collect and re-order messages so that they can be published to the output channel in a specified order.

The Resequencer implementation in Camel uses an xref:manual::expression.adoc[Expression]
as the `Comparator` to re-order the messages.
By using the expression, then the messages
can easily be re-ordered by a message header or another piece of the message.

Camel supports two re-sequencing algorithms:

* xref:batchConfig-eip.adoc[Batch Resequencing] - *Default mode*: collects messages into a batch, sorts the messages and sends them to their output.
* xref:streamConfig-eip.adoc[Stream Resequencing] - re-orders (continuous) message streams based on the detection of gaps between messages.

By default, the Resequencer does not support duplicate messages and will only keep the last message, in case a message arrives with the same message expression.
However, in the batch mode you can enable it to allow duplicates.

== Options

// eip options: START
include::partial$eip-options.adoc[]
// eip options: END

== Exchange properties

// eip exchangeProperties: START
include::partial$eip-exchangeProperties.adoc[]
// eip exchangeProperties: END

== Batch Resequencing

The following example shows how to use the Resequencer in xref:batchConfig-eip.adoc[batch mode] (default),
so that messages are sorted in order of the message body.

That is messages are collected into a batch (either by a maximum number of messages per batch or using a timeout)
then they are sorted, and sent out, to continue being routed.

In the example below, we re-order the message based on the content of the message body.
The default batch modes will collect up to 100 messages per batch, or timeout every second.

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence().body()
    .to("mock:result");
----
+
This is equivalent to:
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(body()).batch()
    .to("mock:result");
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <simple>${body}</simple>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              simple:
                expression: "${body}"
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

The batch resequencer can be further configured via the `size()` and `timeout()` methods:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(body()).batch().size(300).timeout(4000L)
    .to("mock:result")
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <batchConfig batchSize="300" batchTimeout="4000"/>
        <simple>${body}</simple>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              simple:
                expression: "${body}"
            batchConfig:
              batchSize: 300
              batchTimeout: 4000
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

This sets the batch size to 300 and the batch timeout to 4000 ms (by default,
the batch size is 100, and the timeout is 1000 ms).

So the above example will reorder messages  in order of their bodies.
Typically, you'd use a header rather than the body to order things; or maybe a part of the body.
So you could replace this expression with:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(header("mySeqNo"))
    .to("mock:result")
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <header>mySeqNo</header>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              header:
                expression: mySeqNo
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

This reorders messages using a custom sequence number with the header name mySeqNo.

=== Allow Duplicates

When allowing duplicates, then the resequencer retains the duplicate message instead
of keeping only the last duplicated message.

In batch mode, you can turn on duplicates as follows:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(header("mySeqNo")).allowDuplicates()
    .to("mock:result")
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <batchConfig allowDuplicates="true"/>
        <header>mySeqNo</header>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              header:
                expression: mySeqNo
            batchConfig:
              allowDuplicates: "true"
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

=== Reverse Ordering

You can reverse the expression ordering.
By default, the order is based on `0..9,A..Z`,
which would let messages with low numbers be ordered first, and thus also outgoing first.
In some cases, you want to reverse the ordering.

In batch mode, you can turn on reverse as follows:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(header("mySeqNo")).reverse()
    .to("mock:result")
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <batchConfig reverse="true"/>
        <header>mySeqNo</header>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              header:
                expression: mySeqNo
            batchConfig:
              reverse: "true"
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

=== Ignoring invalid messages

The Resequencer throws a `CamelExchangeException` if the incoming Exchange is not valid for the resequencer
such as, the expression cannot be evaluated due to a missing header.

You can ignore these kinds of errors, and let the Resequencer skip the invalid Exchange.

To do this, you do as follows:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(header("seqno")).batch()
        // ignore invalid exchanges (they are discarded)
        .ignoreInvalidExchanges()
    .to("mock:result");
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <batchConfig ignoreInvalidExchanges="true"/>
        <header>seqno</header>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              header:
                expression: seqno
            batchConfig:
              ignoreInvalidExchanges: "true"
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

This option is available for both batch and stream mode.

=== Resequence JMS messages based on JMSPriority

It's now much easier to use the Resequencer to resequence messages from JMS queues based on JMSPriority.
For that to work, you need to use the two new options `allowDuplicates` and `reverse`.

Notice this is *only* possible in the `batch` mode of the Resequencer.

[tabs]
====

Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("jms:queue:foo")
    // sort by JMSPriority by allowing duplicates (the message can have the same JMSPriority)
    // and use reverse ordering so 9 is the first output (most important), and 0 is the last
    // use batch mode and fire every 3rd second
    .resequence(header("JMSPriority")).batch().timeout(3000).allowDuplicates().reverse()
    .to("mock:result");
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="jms:queue:foo"/>
    <resequence>
        <header>JMSPriority</header>
        <batchConfig reverse="true" allowDuplicates="true" batchTimeout="3000"/>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: jms:queue:foo
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              header:
                expression: JMSPriority
            batchConfig:
              reverse: "true"
              allowDuplicates: "true"
              batchTimeout: 3000
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

== Stream Resequencing

In streaming mode, then the Resequencer will send out messages as soon as possible when
a message with the next expected sequence number arrived.

The streaming mode requires the messages to be re-ordered based on integer numeric values
that are ordered 1,2,3...N.

The following example uses the header seqnum for the ordering:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
  .resequence(header("seqnum")).stream()
  .to("mock:result");
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <streamConfig/>
        <header>seqno</header>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              header:
                expression: seqnum
            streamConfig: {}
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

The Resequencer keeps a backlog of pending messages in a backlog.
The default capacity is 1000 elements, which can be configured:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(header("seqnum")).stream().capacity(5000).timeout(4000)
    .to("mock:result")
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <streamConfig capacity="5000" timeout="4000"/>
        <header>seqno</header>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              header:
                expression: seqno
            streamConfig:
              timeout: 4000
              capacity: 5000
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====

This uses a capacity of 5000 elements. And the timeout has been set to 4 seconds.
In case of a timeout, then the resequencer disregards the current expected sequence number,
and moves to the next expected number.

=== How streaming mode works?

The stream-processing resequencer algorithm is based on the detection of gaps in a message stream rather than on a fixed batch size.
Gap detection in combination with timeouts removes the constraint of having to know the number of messages of a sequence (i.e.,
the batch size) in advance.
Messages must contain a unique sequence number for which a predecessor and a successor are known.

For example,
a message with the sequence number 3 has a predecessor message with the sequence number 2 and a successor message with the sequence number 4.
The message sequence 2,3,5 has a gap because the successor of 3 is missing.
The resequencer therefore has to retain message 5 until message 4 arrives (or a timeout occurs).

If the maximum time difference between messages (with successor/predecessor relationship with respect to the sequence number)
in a message stream is known, then the Resequencer timeout parameter should be set to this value.

In this case, it is guaranteed that all messages of a stream are delivered in correct order to the next processor.
The lower the timeout value is compared to the out-of-sequence time difference,
the higher is the probability for out-of-sequence messages delivered by this Resequencer.
Large timeout values should be supported by sufficiently high capacity values.
The capacity parameter is used to prevent the Resequencer from running out of memory.

=== Using custom streaming mode sequence expression

By default, the stream Resequencer expects long sequence numbers,
but other sequence numbers types can be supported as well by providing a custom expression.

[source,java]
----
public class MyFileNameExpression implements Expression {

    public String getFileName(Exchange exchange) {
        return exchange.getIn().getBody(String.class);
    }

    public Object evaluate(Exchange exchange) {
        // parser the file name with YYYYMMDD-DNNN pattern
        String fileName = getFileName(exchange);
        String[] files = fileName.split("-D");
        Long answer = Long.parseLong(files[0]) * 1000 + Long.parseLong(files[1]);
        return answer;
    }

    public <T> T evaluate(Exchange exchange, Class<T> type) {
        Object result = evaluate(exchange);
        return exchange.getContext().getTypeConverter().convertTo(type, result);
    }
}
----

And then you can use this expression in a Camel route:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(new MyFileNameExpression()).stream().timeout(2000)
    .to("mock:result");
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <method beanType="com.foo.MyFileNameExpression"/>
        <streamConfig timeout="2000"/>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
      uri: direct:start
      steps:
        - resequence:
            expression:
              method:
                beanType: com.foo.MyFileNameExpression
            streamConfig:
              timeout: 2000
            steps:
              - to:
                  uri: mock:result
----
====


=== Rejecting old messages

Rejecting old messages is used to prevent out of order messages from being sent,
regardless of the event that delivered messages downstream (capacity, timeout, etc).

If enabled, the Resequencer will throw a `MessageRejectedException`
when an incoming Exchange is _older_ (based on the `Comparator`) than the last message delivered.

This provides an extra level of control in regard to delayed message ordering.

In the example below, old messages are rejected:

[tabs]
====
Java::
+
[source,java]
----
from("direct:start")
    .resequence(header("seqno")).stream().timeout(1000).rejectOld()
    .to("mock:result");
----

XML::
+
[source,xml]
----
<route>
    <from uri="direct:start"/>
    <resequence>
        <streamConfig rejectOld="true" timeout="1000"/>
        <header>seqno</header>
        <to uri="mock:result"/>
    </resequence>
</route>
----

YAML::
+
[source,yaml]
----
- route:
    from:
