1	= Apache Camel 4.x Upgrade Guide
2	
3	This document is for helping you upgrade your Apache Camel application
4	from Camel 4.x to 4.y. For example, if you are upgrading Camel 4.0 to 4.2, then you should follow the guides
5	from both 4.0 to 4.1 and 4.1 to 4.2.
6	
7	[NOTE]
8	====
9	https://github.com/apache/camel-upgrade-recipes/[The Camel Upgrade Recipes project] provides automated assistance for some common migration tasks.
10	Note that manual migration is still required.
11	See the xref:camel-upgrade-recipes-tool.adoc[documentation] page for details.
12	====
13	
14	== Upgrading Camel 4.20 to 4.21
15	
16	=== camel-grok - potential breaking change
17	
18	The library used for Camel Grok component has been migrated from no more maintained `io.krakens:java-grok` to its fork `io.github.whatap:java-grok`.
19	It implies small differences listed https://github.com/whatap/java-grok#what-is-different-from-iokrakensjava-grok[here].
20	
21	=== camel-jbang
22	
23	The project exported by `camel export` now includes additional guidance for AI coding assistants. The generated
24	`readme.md` gained a _For AI coding assistants_ section linking to the Apache Camel website LLM index,
25	and a new `AGENTS.md` file is generated at the project root. This applies to all runtimes (Camel Main, Spring Boot and Quarkus).
26	
27	=== camel-micrometer
28	
29	The `MicrometerExchangeEventNotifier` now always includes the `routeId` tag on exchange event metrics.
30	When `fromRouteId` is not available (for example, on Exchanges created by `ProducerTemplate`), the tag value is an empty string instead of omitting the tag.
31	This ensures Prometheus-compatible metric export when Route triggered and ProducerTemplate Exchanges are mixed.
32	Dashboards or alerts that assumed the `routeId` label was absent must be updated to treat `routeId=""` accordingly.
33	
34	=== camel-dataformat
35	
36	The `dataformat:` component now applies the global `camel.dataformat.<name>.*` configuration (for example from
37	`application.properties`) when it creates the data format instance. Previously a `dataformat:` endpoint such as
38	`to("dataformat:beanio:unmarshal")` ignored this global configuration and used only the options provided on the
39	endpoint URI, which could leave the data format incompletely configured. This aligns the component with the
40	`marshal()` / `unmarshal()` DSL, which already honors the global configuration. Options specified on the endpoint URI
41	continue to take precedence over the global configuration.
42	
43	=== camel-langchain4j-tools
44	
45	When the LLM requests multiple tool invocations within a single request, each tool is now invoked on its
46	own independent copy of the exchange (similar to the multicast/splitter patterns) instead of sharing a single
47	exchange across all tools. This guarantees complete isolation: the message body, argument headers, and any
48	exception produced by one tool no longer leak into subsequent tool invocations of the same request.
49	
50	As a consequence, a tool route no longer receives the previous tool's output as its message body, nor the
51	previous tool's argument headers. Each tool route starts from the original incoming exchange and only sees
52	its own declared arguments as headers. Routes that (intentionally or accidentally) relied on this leaked state
53	between tools must be adjusted to carry such data explicitly.
54	
55	=== Component documentation
56	
57	The Spring Boot auto-configuration tables have been removed from individual component documentation pages.
58	Spring Boot auto-configuration options are now documented in the Camel Spring Boot documentation site,
59	with a dedicated page per starter.
60	
61	=== camel-core
62	
63	==== Simple language: internal builder classes reorganized
64	
65	The internal class `org.apache.camel.language.simple.SimpleExpressionBuilder`
66	(in `camel-core-languages`) has been removed. Its methods have been split across
67	seven domain-aligned builder classes:
68	
69	- `CollectionExpressionBuilder`
70	- `DateExpressionBuilder`
71	- `FileExpressionBuilder`
72	- `MathExpressionBuilder`
73	- `MiscExpressionBuilder`
74	- `OgnlExpressionBuilder`
75	- `StringExpressionBuilder`
76	
77	These classes are all in the `org.apache.camel.language.simple` package and are
78	internal API (not part of `camel-api`). Applications and custom DSLs that import
79	`SimpleExpressionBuilder` directly must update their imports to the appropriate
80	replacement class.
81	
82	==== Simple language: `floor`/`ceil` return `long` instead of `int`
83	
84	The `floor` and `ceil` functions in the Simple language previously silently
85	overflowed for input values larger than `Integer.MAX_VALUE` because the result
86	was cast to `int`. They now return `long`. Routes that pass the result of
87	`floor`/`ceil` to a method that expects `int` may require an explicit cast or
88	a converter step.
89	
90	==== Simple language: `range(N)` starts at 1, not 0
91	
92	In csimple, `range(N)` previously generated a range starting at 0 (i.e., 0..N-1).
93	It now starts at 1 (i.e., 1..N), consistent with the `simple` language behaviour.
94	Routes using csimple with `range(N)` that relied on the range starting at 0 must
95	be updated.
96	
97	The `camel-api` module now has an optional dependency on `org.jspecify:jspecify` for null safety annotations
98	(`@NullMarked`, `@Nullable`). The dependency is `<optional>true</optional>`, so it is not pulled transitively
99	into your application. The annotations are used to document nullability contracts across the Camel API surface
100	and enable compile-time null checking with tools like NullAway. If you want to leverage these annotations in
101	your own code or tooling, add `org.jspecify:jspecify` explicitly to your project dependencies.
102	
103	The `org.apache.camel.support.DefaultHeaderFilterStrategy` changed default setting for lowercase from `false` to `true`.
104	If your application relies on case-sensitive header name matching (for example, two headers that differ only in
105	case such as `X-Foo` and `x-foo`), restore the previous behavior by calling `setLowerCase(false)` on your strategy.
106	
107	The `DefaultHeaderFilterStrategy` now blocks headers whose names start with `Camel` or `camel`
108	(case-insensitive) by default in both the inbound (external to Camel) and outbound
109	(Camel to external) directions. Previously this required each component to explicitly call
110	`setInFilterStartsWith(CAMEL_FILTER_STARTS_WITH)` and `setOutFilterStartsWith(CAMEL_FILTER_STARTS_WITH)`.
111	
112	This affects anyone who:
113	
114	* Configures `DefaultHeaderFilterStrategy` directly, OR
115	* Extends `DefaultHeaderFilterStrategy` in a custom component without explicitly calling `setInFilterStartsWith` or `setOutFilterStartsWith`.
116	
117	If these headers need to pass through, opt out by calling `setInFilterStartsWith((String[]) null)` and/or
118	`setOutFilterStartsWith((String[]) null)`, or by supplying a custom filter array.
119	See `ClassicJmsHeaderFilterStrategy` for a worked example of the opt-out pattern.
120	
121	The following component-specific header filter strategy classes have been removed because
122	`DefaultHeaderFilterStrategy` now provides equivalent behavior by default:
123	
124	* `CoAPHeaderFilterStrategy`
125	* `CometdHeaderFilterStrategy`
126	* `IggyHeaderFilterStrategy`
127	* `NatsHeaderFilterStrategy`
128	* `VertxWebsocketHeaderFilterStrategy`
129	* `XmppHeaderFilterStrategy`
130	
131	If your code references any of these classes directly, replace them with `new DefaultHeaderFilterStrategy()`, which now provides the same behavior by default.
132	
133	The class `org.apache.camel.http.common.HttpProtocolHeaderFilterStrategy` has been deprecated.
134	Use `org.apache.camel.http.base.HttpProtocolHeaderFilterStrategy` directly instead.
135	
136	==== Error Registry SPI changes
137	
138	The `ErrorRegistry` SPI has been enhanced to capture rich exchange data snapshots at error time,
139	following the same pattern as BacklogTracer. The following breaking changes apply:
140	
141	- The `org.apache.camel.spi.ErrorRegistryEntry` interface has been removed and replaced by `BacklogErrorEventMessage`,
142	  which extends a new `BacklogEventMessage` base interface shared with `BacklogTracerEventMessage`.
143	- The `ErrorRegistryView.browse()` methods now return `Collection<BacklogErrorEventMessage>` instead of
144	  `Collection<ErrorRegistryEntry>`.
145	- The `stackTraceEnabled` option on `ErrorRegistry` has been removed. The full `Throwable` is now always captured.
146	- Error registry configuration properties have moved from `camel.main.errorRegistryXxx` to the grouped
147	  `camel.errorRegistry.*` prefix. The old flat properties (`camel.main.errorRegistryEnabled`,
148	  `camel.main.errorRegistryMaximumEntries`, `camel.main.errorRegistryTimeToLiveSeconds`,
149	  `camel.main.errorRegistryStackTraceEnabled`) are removed.
150	
151	New configuration properties under `camel.errorRegistry.*`:
152	
153	[options="header"]
154	|===
155	| Old property | New property
156	| `camel.main.errorRegistryEnabled` | `camel.errorRegistry.enabled`
157	| `camel.main.errorRegistryMaximumEntries` | `camel.errorRegistry.maximumEntries`
158	| `camel.main.errorRegistryTimeToLiveSeconds` | `camel.errorRegistry.timeToLiveSeconds`
159	| `camel.main.errorRegistryStackTraceEnabled` | _(removed, always captures full exception)_
160	|===
161	
162	New capture options: `bodyMaxChars`, `bodyIncludeStreams`, `bodyIncludeFiles`,
163	`includeExchangeProperties`, `includeExchangeVariables` — matching the BacklogTracer configuration.
164	
165	==== Virtual threads: `maxQueueSize` now honored in `threads()` EIP
166	
167	When virtual threads are enabled (`camel.threads.virtual.enabled=true`), the `threads()` EIP now honors `maxQueueSize`
168	for backpressure. Previously, `maxQueueSize` was silently ignored and tasks were accepted unboundedly.
169	
170	The virtual thread executor is wrapped with a semaphore-based concurrency limit (`BoundedExecutorService`) that enforces
171	a flat cap of `maxQueueSize` on delegated tasks. The `keepAliveTime` parameter is repurposed as the
172	semaphore acquisition timeout (pool sizing parameters are not applicable to virtual threads).
173	
174	==== New `Block` rejected policy
175	
176	A new `Block` value has been added to `ThreadPoolRejectedPolicy`. With `Block`, the caller blocks indefinitely until
177	capacity becomes available. This applies to both platform and virtual threads. The existing `CallerRuns` (default) and
178	`Abort` policies are unchanged.
179	
180	The type converters for Java serialized objects with types `java.io.ObjectInput` and `java.io.ObjectOutput` has been removed.
181	Java object serialization is a recurring source of security issues and therefore these converters has been removed.
182	These converters are not used at all by Camel itself. To restore compatibility then end users can add these type converters back as custom converters in their own Camel applications.
183	However, using Java serialization is discouraged and highly recommend to use other means.
184	
185	Removed `org.apache.camel.spi.ReifierStrategy` which is no longer in use by Camel and can safely be removed.
186	
187	A new `getChildren()` default method has been added to `org.apache.camel.NamedNode`. It returns `List<NamedNode>` and
188	provides a uniform API for traversing the route model tree, including structural nodes like `WhenDefinition` and
189	`OtherwiseDefinition` that are not `ProcessorDefinition` subclasses and therefore invisible to `getOutputs()`.
190	Code that walks the model tree recursively should prefer `getChildren()` over `getOutputs()` to avoid needing
191	special-case handling for Choice EIP.
192	
193	The `NamedNode.getLevel()` and `getParentId()` methods no longer treat `when` and `otherwise` nodes as "shallow".
194	Previously these nodes were flattened to the same level as their parent `choice`, which was inconsistent with how
195	`doCatch` and `doFinally` work inside `doTry`. Now `when` and `otherwise` contribute their own level, consistent
196	with the tree structure exposed by `getChildren()`. This affects level values reported via JMX (`ManagedProcessor.getLevel()`)
197	and dev consoles for nodes inside Choice EIP branches.
198	
199	=== camel-main
200	
201	The development-only self-signed certificate generated when `camel.ssl.selfSigned=true` (used to enable HTTPS without
202	providing a keystore) now defaults to an *EC* key (NIST P-256, signed with SHA256withECDSA) instead of *RSA-2048*.
203	The key algorithm is configurable via the new `camel.ssl.selfSignedKeyType` option, which accepts `EC` (default) or
204	`RSA`. Set `camel.ssl.selfSignedKeyType=RSA` to retain the previous behaviour.
205	
206	This certificate is intended for development use only and is regenerated on each restart, so the change does not affect
207	production deployments, which must provide their own keystore via `camel.ssl.keyStore`.
208	
209	=== camel-jbang
210	
211	Camel JBang has been rebranded as Camel CLI in the documentation.
212	Camel CLI is powered by https://www.jbang.dev/[JBang].
213	
214	README files (`README.md`, `README.adoc`, etc.) are now included when running integrations with `camel run`.
215	Previously these files were silently excluded. The README files are added to the classpath
216	and exposed via the CLI connector so tools such as `camel monitor` can display them.
217	
218	The `camel wrapper` command now installs the scripts as `camel` instead of `camelw`.
219	You can use the `--command-name=camelw` to use the old name.
220	
221	The `camel run` and `camel export` commands now auto-detect `application.properties` (and profile-specific
222	variants such as `application-prod.properties`) in the current directory and include them automatically.
223	Previously you had to explicitly list the properties file on the command line
224	(e.g. `camel run hello.java application.properties`). This is no longer required.
225	
226	When `camel.main.routesReloadEnabled=true` (automatically set by `camel run --dev`), Camel now
227	auto-disables `contentCache` on resource-based components (such as `xslt`) whose default is
228	`true`, so that edits to the resource file are picked up on the next message without restarting
229	the route. Set `camel.component.<name>.contentCache=true` (or pass `?contentCache=true` on the
230	URI) to opt back in to caching during dev mode.
231	
232	The `camel get route-dump` command has been moved to `camel cmd route-dump`, grouping it with the other
233	route action commands (`route-diagram`, `route-structure`, `route-topology`).
234	
235	==== Container-optimized layered Docker packaging for `camel export`
236	
237	The `camel export` command now generates Dockerfiles optimized for container image layer caching
238	across all three runtimes. Previously, Camel Main and Spring Boot exports produced a single-layer
239	fat JAR Dockerfile; now each runtime uses a layered approach where dependencies (stable) and
240	application code (volatile) are in separate Docker layers.
241	
242	Each runtime implements layered packaging using its native mechanism:
243	
244	- **Camel Main**: the generated POM now includes `maven-jar-plugin` (with classpath manifest) and
245	  `maven-dependency-plugin:copy-dependencies`. The Dockerfile copies `target/lib/` first (cached
246	  dependency layer), then the thin `.original` JAR (small application layer).
247	- **Spring Boot**: a multi-stage Dockerfile using Spring Boot's `jarmode=tools extract --layers`
248	  to split the fat JAR into 4 Docker layers (dependencies, spring-boot-loader,
249	  snapshot-dependencies, application).
250	- **Quarkus**: unchanged — already uses fast-jar packaging with a 4-layer Dockerfile.
251	
252	The Quarkus-specific `--quarkus-package-type` option is now deprecated and hidden from help output.
253	Quarkus exports always use fast-jar (layered) packaging.
254	
255	==== Improved default `--quarkus-version`
256	
257	The default behavior of commands requiring Quarkus Platform version has improved.
258	Before Camel 4.21.0, when the version was not set via `--quarkus-version`, a default hardcoded value was used.
259	We were not especially good at upgrading that version and also, the version of Quarkus Platform that delivers
260	a specific version of Camel was always released several weeks after Camel, so it could never be up to date.
261	
262	Since Camel 4.21.0, the default version of Quarkus Platform is determined at Camel JBang runtime by querying
263	Quarkus Extension Registry (https://registry.quarkus.io/), especially its
264	`https://registry.quarkus.io/client/platforms/all[/client/platforms/all]` endpoint.
265	The version chosen is the newest Quarkus Platform compatible with either the Camel version specified via `--camel-version`,
266	if available, or the version of the currently running `camel-jbang`.
267	
268	Unless you pass `--fresh`, the content from Quarkus Extension Registry is fetched at most once a day
269	and cached under `~/.camel/quarkus-extension-registries`.
270	You may force using the cached resource by passing `--download=false`.
271	
272	Finally, the default base URI `https://registry.quarkus.io` of Quarkus Extension Registry
273	can be overridden via `--quarkus-ext-registry`.
274	In addition to `http://` and `https://`, it also accepts `file://` URIs.
275	Those may come in handy when cannot access the internet.
276	Append `.json` to the resource path when you store it locally.
277	
278	The internal Maven downloader used by Camel JBang now defaults to preferring locally cached
279	artifacts over remote ones (`preferLocal = true`). Previously the default was `false`, meaning
280	the downloader would always check remote repositories first. With the new default, already-resolved
281	artifacts in the local Maven repository are used directly without contacting remote repositories.
282	Use `--fresh` to force re-downloading from remote repositories when needed.
283	
284	==== camel-jbang-mcp
285	
286	The `camel_route_context` tool response no longer echoes the input route back to the caller. The
287	`route` field has been removed from `RouteContextResult`; the response now contains only `format`,
288	`components`, `eips`, and `summary`. This reduces token consumption for callers passing large
289	routes.
290	
291	The `camel_catalog_component_doc` tool no longer returns every component option by default. Two new
292	arguments make the response lean by default to reduce LLM context-window pressure:
293	
294	* `optionsFilter` (optional) — case-insensitive substring match on option name.
295	* `includeOptions` (optional) — one of `required`, `common`, or `all`; defaults to `common`, which
296	  excludes deprecated options and options whose label contains `advanced`. Pass `all` to restore the
297	  previous behaviour.
298	
299	The `groupId`, `artifactId`, and `version` fields have been removed from the `camel_catalog_component_doc`
300	response. A new `camel_catalog_component_maven` tool returns just those Maven coordinates and should be
301	called only when you actually need to add the component as a dependency.
302	
303	The list-style catalog tools — `camel_catalog_components`, `camel_catalog_dataformats`,
304	`camel_catalog_languages`, and `camel_catalog_eips` — no longer return the verbose
305	`description` field in their per-item entries. Callers that need the full description should call
306	the matching `*_doc` tool. The default `limit` for `camel_catalog_components` and
307	`camel_catalog_dataformats` is also lowered from 50 to 20; pass an explicit `limit` to restore the
308	previous behaviour.
309	
310	==== camel-jbang plugin commands
311	
312	The `camel plugin get --all` option is deprecated. Use the new `camel plugin list` command instead,
313	which lists all available plugins (installed, bundled, and known 3rd party).
314	The `camel plugin get` command now only shows installed plugins.
315	
316	==== camel-jbang plugins
317	
318	Plugins are now loaded lazily. Built-in commands that do not consume plugins
319	(for example `camel get`, `camel version`, `camel ps`, `camel stop`) skip plugin
320	discovery entirely, avoiding classpath scans and Maven resolution on every
321	invocation. Plugin-consuming commands (`run`, `export`, `cmd`, `shell`) and
322	plugin-provided commands (such as `kubernetes`, `generate`, `test`) continue
323	to work unchanged. When an external plugin is resolved through Maven, its resolved classpath is
324	cached in `~/.camel-jbang-plugins.json` under a new `resolved` block. Subsequent
325	invocations load the plugin directly from the cached jars without going through
326	the Maven downloader. The cache is validated by file size and modification time
327	on both the cached jars and the plugin's POM, so SNAPSHOT plugins rebuilt
328	locally are picked up automatically. The cache is also invalidated when the
329	Camel version, the plugin GAV, or the effective `--repos`/`--repo` value
330	changes. No user action is required; existing plugin entries are populated on
331	first use after upgrade.
332	
333	=== camel-opentelemetry2 — Embedded OTLP receiver for OpenTelemetry Java Agent
334	
335	A new `exportTarget` option has been added to `OpenTelemetryTracer`. When using the OpenTelemetry Java Agent
336	via Camel JBang, this option controls where spans are exported:
337	
338	* `tui` (default) — an embedded OTLP receiver is started inside the Camel process; the agent exports spans
339	  to this receiver, which feeds them into the TUI Spans tab. No external collector is needed.
340	* `jaeger` — the agent exports spans directly to a Jaeger instance (OTLP on port 4318). No embedded
341	  receiver is created.
342	
343	Two new CLI flags have been added to `camel run`, `camel dev`, and `camel debug`:
344	
345	* `--open-telemetry-agent` — attaches the OpenTelemetry Java Agent to the Camel process. The agent
346	  auto-instruments HTTP clients, JDBC, Kafka clients, gRPC, and other libraries.
347	* `--open-telemetry-agent-export=<tui|jaeger>` — selects the export target (default: `tui`).
348	
349	The `camel-opentelemetry2` module now has an additional `provided`-scope dependency on
350	`io.opentelemetry.proto:opentelemetry-proto` for the embedded OTLP receiver. This dependency is not
351	pulled transitively into applications using `camel-opentelemetry2` at runtime.
352	
353	=== camel-yaml-dsl
354	
355	A new canonical JSON Schema variant (`camelYamlDsl-canonical.json`) has been added alongside the existing classic
356	schema (`camelYamlDsl.json`). The canonical schema removes all implicit patterns (string shorthands, inline
357	expressions, `oneOf`/`anyOf`/`not` constructs) to provide a simpler, more predictable schema for tooling
358	such as IDEs, code generators, and AI assistants. See the xref:components:others:yaml-dsl.adoc[YAML DSL]
359	documentation for details.
360	
361	The `YamlValidator` class now accepts a `boolean canonical` constructor parameter to validate against the
362	canonical schema.
363	
364	A new `camel validate normalize` command has been added to Camel CLI. It rewrites YAML routes from the
365	classic (shorthand) form to the canonical (explicit) form. The `camel validate yaml` command also
366	supports a new `--canonical` flag to validate against the canonical schema.
367	
368	Camel now logs a WARN message when YAML DSL routes use compact (shorthand) notation instead of
369	the canonical form. This encourages adopting the canonical style which is more tooling and AI friendly.
370	The warning is logged once per resource file. To disable this warning, set
371	`camel.main.yamlDslCompactNotationWarn = false` in `application.properties`.
372	
373	The YAML DSL now supports classpath-discovered `YamlDeserializerResolver` implementations.
374	Optional modules can list resolver classes in `META-INF/services/org/apache/camel/YamlDeserializerResolver`
375	to contribute custom YAML route step names automatically when the module is on the classpath, without changing
376	`camel-yaml-dsl` and without requiring users to register resolver beans before routes are parsed.
377	Registry-provided resolvers remain supported.
378	Custom step names are runtime extensions and are not included in the generated YAML DSL JSON schemas unless schema
379	generation is extended separately.
380	See xref:components:others:yaml-dsl.adoc[YAML DSL] for details.
381	
382	=== camel-a2a (new, preview)
383	
384	A new `camel-a2a` component has been added providing support for the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol.
385	The component scheme is `a2a`. As a consumer, it exposes Camel routes as A2A-compliant agents;
386	as a producer, it calls remote A2A agents. This component has **Preview** support level.
387	The component also provides an `a2aSubTask` route step for scoped progress updates around nested route steps in Camel's model-based DSLs.
388	This is a generated Camel route model step available to DSLs such as Java, XML, and YAML; it is not a component-provided YAML deserializer extension.
389	Routes that use it need `camel-a2a` on the classpath for runtime processor creation.
390	The progress expressions are route semantics, so expression evaluation failures fail the route.
391	Failures while storing or notifying the resulting progress event are best-effort and do not replace the route outcome.
392	Progress emission outside an active A2A task context is a no-op by default; set `failIfNoTaskContext=true` on
393	`a2aSubTask` when such use should fail route processing.
394	
395	Consumer routes require an HTTP server component on the classpath. Use `camel-platform-http-vertx` for JSON-RPC agents, or use `camel-undertow` / `camel-jetty` when serving REST custom-method routes such as `/message:send` or real-time SSE streaming. The default Vert.x/platform-http transport treats colons as path-parameter delimiters and buffers `InputStream` streaming responses.
396	
397	For A2A consumers, `validateAuth` defaults to `true`. The well-known agent card endpoint remains public, but A2A
398	operation endpoints fail closed unless the resolved agent card declares a security scheme that Camel can validate.
399	Set `validateAuth=false` explicitly only when intentionally serving unauthenticated A2A operations. Network-exposed agents should declare `securitySchemes` and `securityRequirements` in the agent card and configure `apiKey`, `bearerToken`, or `oauthProfile` on the endpoint.
400	
401	=== camel-kafka / Spring Boot
402	
403	When using `camel-kafka-starter` with Spring Boot, the standard `spring.kafka.*` properties are now automatically
404	bridged to the Camel Kafka component configuration (CAMEL-22760). This means you no longer need to duplicate
405	Kafka settings under both `spring.kafka.*` and `camel.component.kafka.*`.
406	
407	The bridged properties include `bootstrap-servers`, `security.protocol`, SSL/TLS settings (keystore, truststore),
408	`consumer.group-id`, `client-id`, and SASL properties (`sasl.mechanism`, `sasl.jaas.config`, `sasl.kerberos.service.name`).
409	
410	Explicit `camel.component.kafka.*` settings always take precedence over the bridged Spring Boot values.
411	
412	The bridge is enabled by default. To disable it, set:
413	
414	[source,properties]
415	----
416	camel.component.kafka.bridge-spring-kafka-properties=false
417	----
418	
419	=== Default deserialization filter tightened
420	
421	The default `ObjectInputFilter` pattern that ships with the components listed below has been tightened
422	to explicitly deny classes under `java.net.**` before allowing the rest of `java.**`, `javax.**` and
423	`org.apache.camel.**`. The previous default did not deny `java.net.**`, which meant classes whose
424	`hashCode`/`equals` methods perform network I/O (notably `java.net.URL` and `java.net.InetAddress`)
425	could be deserialized by the in-code default.
426	
427	Affected components:
428	
429	* `camel-jms`, `camel-sjms`, `camel-amqp`, `camel-mina`, `camel-netty`, `camel-netty-http`,
430	`camel-vertx-http`, `camel-infinispan`
431	* The aggregation repository components: `camel-leveldb`, `camel-cassandraql`, `camel-consul`,
432	`camel-sql` (JDBC aggregation repository)
433	
434	The new default is:
435	
436	[source,text]
437	----
438	!java.net.**;java.**;javax.**;org.apache.camel.**;!*
439	----
440	
441	(or `!java.net.**;java.**;org.apache.camel.**;!*` for the aggregation repository components, which
442	do not include `javax.**`).
443	
444	The endpoint-level option `deserializationFilter` and the JVM-wide system property
445	`-Djdk.serialFilter` continue to override this default. Applications that have a legitimate need to
446	deserialize `java.net.URL` or other `java.net.*` types must configure an explicit filter.
447	
448	For production deployments handling untrusted serialized payloads, the in-code filter is intended as
449	defense-in-depth only. The primary mitigation should be configured at the messaging provider:
450	
451	* ActiveMQ Artemis: `deserializationAllowList` / `deserializationDenyList` (see the Artemis docs)
452	* ActiveMQ Classic: the `org.apache.activemq.SERIALIZABLE_PACKAGES` system property
453	
454	=== camel-jms
455	
456	JMS `ObjectMessage` support is now disabled by default. Java object serialization is a recurring source
457	of security issues, and Camel JMS routes rarely use `ObjectMessage` in practice. The component will now
458	refuse to create or read `jakarta.jms.ObjectMessage` instances unless the new `objectMessageEnabled`
459	option is explicitly set to `true`.
460	
461	This affects the following endpoint/component options that rely on `ObjectMessage` internally:
462	
463	* `jmsMessageType=Object` (or sending a `Serializable` body that is auto-detected as `Object`)
464	* `transferExchange=true`
465	* `transferException=true`
466	* receiving a JMS `ObjectMessage` produced by an external sender
467	
468	To restore the previous behavior, enable the option at the component or endpoint level:
469	
470	[source,properties]
471	----
472	camel.component.jms.objectMessageEnabled=true
473	----
474	
475	Or, on a single endpoint:
476	
477	[source,text]
478	----
479	jms:queue:foo?objectMessageEnabled=true
480	----
481	
482	=== camel-nats
483	
484	The NATS consumer no longer sends a reply to the `replyTo` subject by default. Previously, the consumer
485	would unconditionally publish the exchange body back to the NATS `replyTo` subject after route processing,
486	regardless of the exchange pattern. This could cause `TypeConversionException` or payload-size-exceeded
487	errors when the route body was not suitable for reply.
488	
489	The reply is now only sent when the exchange pattern is `InOut`. To restore request/reply behavior, set
490	`exchangePattern=InOut` on the consumer endpoint:
491	
492	[source,text]
493	----
494	nats:myTopic?exchangePattern=InOut
495	----
496	
497	Routes that do not use NATS request/reply are unaffected.
498	
499	=== camel-sjms / camel-sjms2
500	
501	The same default applies to `camel-sjms` (and `camel-sjms2`, which inherits from it): JMS `ObjectMessage`
502	support is now disabled by default and gated by a new `objectMessageEnabled` option (default `false`)
503	on `SjmsComponent` / `SjmsEndpoint`.
504	
505	This affects the same endpoint/component options as `camel-jms`:
506	
507	* `jmsMessageType=Object` (or sending a `Serializable` body that is auto-detected as `Object`)
508	* `transferException=true`
509	* receiving a JMS `ObjectMessage` produced by an external sender
510	
511	To restore the previous behavior, enable the option at the component or endpoint level:
512	
513	[source,properties]
514	----
515	camel.component.sjms.objectMessageEnabled=true
516	camel.component.sjms2.objectMessageEnabled=true
517	----
518	
519	Or, on a single endpoint:
520	
521	[source,text]
522	----
523	sjms:queue:foo?objectMessageEnabled=true
524	sjms2:queue:foo?objectMessageEnabled=true
525	----
526	
527	=== camel-redis
528	
529	The `RedisAggregationRepository` now uses per-key distributed locks instead of a single global lock.
530	Previously, all pessimistic aggregation operations serialized on a single Redis lock named `"aggregationLock"`.
531	The lock key is now `<mapName>-lock-<correlationKey>`, so operations on unrelated correlation keys no longer
532	block each other. Existing deployments that inspect Redis lock keys will see different key names after upgrading.
533	
534	The `remove()` method now throws `RuntimeCamelException` instead of `RuntimeException` when a transaction
535	rollback occurs. Routes that catch `RuntimeException` explicitly are unaffected, but routes that catch
536	`RuntimeCamelException` specifically will now also catch this failure path.
537	
538	=== camel-hazelcast
539	
540	Hazelcast instances created and managed by Camel (when no user-supplied
541	`Config` or `HazelcastInstance` is provided) now apply a default
542	`JavaSerializationFilterConfig` on the `SerializationConfig` of the
543	`Config` built by Camel. The default whitelists the class name prefixes
544	`java.`, `javax.`, `org.apache.camel.` and blacklists `java.net.`.
545	
546	This affects:
547	
548	* `camel-hazelcast` component endpoints when neither `hazelcastInstance`,
549	`hazelcastConfigUri`, nor a referenced `Config` is supplied
550	* `HazelcastAggregationRepository` and `HazelcastIdempotentRepository`
551	when no `hazelcastInstance` is supplied
552	* `HazelcastUtil#newInstance()` (no-arg)
553	
554	A user-supplied `JavaSerializationFilterConfig` (set on the
555	`SerializationConfig` of a `Config` provided via `hazelcastConfigUri`, a
556	referenced `Config` bean, or already wired into a pre-built
557	`HazelcastInstance`) is respected and is not overwritten.
558	
559	Applications that store classes outside the default whitelist on a
560	Hazelcast topic, queue, map, list, set, or in one of the repositories
561	above must provide their own `Config` with a
562	`JavaSerializationFilterConfig` configured for their class names.
563	
564	=== camel-bindy
565	
566	Bindy can now keep going when an individual field fails to parse, instead of aborting the whole
567	unmarshal. Two new opt-in annotation elements drive this:
568	
569	* `continueParseOnFailure` on `@CsvRecord`, `@FixedLengthRecord`, and `@Message` (record-level boolean,
570	  default `false`). When `true`, every field on the record tolerates parse failures by default.
571	* `continueParseOnFailure` on `@DataField` and `@KeyValuePairField` (field-level tri-state of type
572	  `ContinueOnFailure`, default `INHERIT`). `TRUE` and `FALSE` override the record-level setting
573	  for that one field; `INHERIT` defers to the record.
574	
575	When a parse fails and the field is tolerant, Bindy substitutes the field's existing `@DataField.defaultValue`
576	(if set) by running it through the same `Format` instance — so the substitute is automatically the
577	right Java type. If no `defaultValue` is set, the field gets the same fallback an unfilled field would
578	get today: `null` for object types, and the primitive's `MIN_VALUE` sentinel (the existing
579	`getDefaultValueForPrimitive` convention) for `int`, `long`, etc. `boolean` falls back to `false` and
580	`String` to `""` (or `null` if `defaultValueStringAsNull` is enabled on the DataFormat).
581	
582	Everything is opt-in; default behavior is unchanged. `@KeyValuePairField` does not have a `defaultValue`
583	element today, so KVP fields can only fall back to the type-appropriate default.
584	
585	[source,java]
586	----
587	@CsvRecord(separator = ",", continueParseOnFailure = true)
588	public class Order {
589	    @DataField(pos = 1)
590	    private int id;
591	
592	    @DataField(pos = 2, pattern = "yyyy-MM-dd", defaultValue = "1970-01-01")
593	    private Date orderDate;     // bad input -> 1970-01-01 instead of throwing
594	
595	    @DataField(pos = 3, continueParseOnFailure = ContinueOnFailure.FALSE)
596	    private BigDecimal amount;  // this one field stays strict
597	}
598	----
599	
600	=== camel-keycloak
601	
602	The `KeycloakSecurityPolicy` route policy now always verifies the access token when one is present - signature,
603	issuer and expiry for local JWT verification, or active state and issuer when token introspection is enabled -
604	even when neither `requiredRoles` nor `requiredPermissions` is configured. Previously the token was only verified
605	when at least one role or permission was required.
606	
607	Routes that attach a `KeycloakSecurityPolicy` without any roles or permissions and that previously forwarded an
608	unverified or invalid token will now have such requests rejected with a `CamelAuthorizationException`. Provide a
609	valid, verifiable token (or configure `requiredRoles` / `requiredPermissions`) for these routes.
610	
611	=== camel-elasticsearch / camel-elasticsearch-rest-client / camel-solr
612	
613	The `camel-elasticsearch`, `camel-elasticsearch-rest-client` and `camel-solr` components now support
614	configuring TLS through `SSLContextParameters` (the JSSE utility), in addition to the existing
615	`certificatePath` option. The new `sslContextParameters` option allows configuring named groups,
616	signature schemes, cipher suites and protocols, for example post-quantum named groups such as
617	`X25519MLKEM768` on JDK 25+. It can be set at the endpoint or component level, and the components also
618	honor the context-wide global SSL configuration via `useGlobalSslContextParameters`. When configured,
619	`SSLContextParameters` takes precedence over `certificatePath`.
620	
621	For `camel-elasticsearch` and `camel-elasticsearch-rest-client`, the HTTP client is now configured with
622	the TLS settings regardless of whether basic authentication (`user`/`password`) is set. Previously the
623	`certificatePath` option was silently ignored unless both `user` and `password` were configured. Routes
624	that set `certificatePath` without credentials will now correctly use the configured TLS context where it
625	was previously a no-op.
626	
627	=== camel-openai
628	
629	The `chat-completion` operation now accepts `WrappedFile` (the body type produced by the file, FTP, SFTP and SMB
630	components), `Path`, `byte[]` and `InputStream` message bodies for file-based and vision model input, in addition
631	to `java.io.File`. Previously such bodies were converted to a String, which corrupted binary image content.
632	
633	The MIME type of the body is now detected from headers (the new `CamelOpenAIMediaType` header, `CamelFileContentType`,
634	cloud storage content-type headers such as `CamelAwsS3ContentType`, or `Content-Type`), and finally the file name
635	extension using the Camel built-in MIME type table. Previously only `Files.probeContentType()` was used, which could
636	fail to detect the type on some platforms. A `byte[]` or `InputStream` body whose detected MIME type is an image is now
637	sent to the model as a base64-encoded image instead of being converted to text; bodies without image MIME type metadata
638	keep the previous text behavior. XML and JSON files (`application/xml`, `application/json`) are treated as text prompts.
639	
640	The `sse` `transportType` is deprecated. it is recommended to switch to `streamableHttp`.
641	
642	=== camel-stomp removal
643	
644	Camel stomp was deprecated with Camel 4.17. The stomp library didn't have any activities in the last 10 years. The component is now removed.
645	
646	=== camel-aws-xray removal
647	
648	Camel AWS X-Ray was deprecated with Camel 4.17. Amazon Web Services X-Ray service is in maintenance mode since February 2026. The component is now removed.
649	
650	=== camel-guava-eventbus removal
651	
652	Camel Guava EventBus was deprecated with Camel 4.6. The component is now removed.
653	
654	=== camel-grape removal
655	
656	Camel Grape was deprecated with Camel 4.1. The component is now removed.
657	
658	=== camel-elytron removal
659	
660	Camel Grape was deprecated with Camel 4.0. The component is now removed.
661	
662	=== camel-github removal
663	
664	Camel GitHub was deprecated with Camel 4.18. The component is now removed.
665	There is `camel-github2` to replace it.
666	Technically, `camel-github` was based on Eclipse EGit which is no more active. `camel-github2` is based on `org.kohsuke:github-api`.
667	
668	=== camel-telemetry
669	
670	In order to prevent some potential span leakage in `camel-opentelemetry2` (see notes below) we had to perform some changes and introduce the following parameter:
671	
672	```
673	|`disableCoreProcessors`| false | Disable any inner core processors (any core DSL processor provided in the route, for example `bean`, `log`, ...).
674	```
675	
676	This is required in order to distinguish the older `traceProcessors` parameter which now accounts **exclusively** for custom processors. Keeping the default parameter (`false`) could increase the number of spans generated, though, this is required to guarantee consistency and memory sealing. If you need to reduce the number of spans for any reason you can explicitly provide exclusion configuration.
677	
678	NOTE: turn `disableCoreProcessors` to `true` and `traceProcessors` to `false` to skip entirely all processors. However it is not recommended to disable core processors in order to avoid the generation of third party dependencies orphaned spans.
679	
680	In this version we have also changed certain interfaces, so, if you have custom telemetry implementations, you may need to adjust to them:
681	
682	==== `org.apache.camel.telemetry.SpanLifecycleManager`
683	
684	The `create` method signature has changed:
685	
686	[source,java]
687	----
688	// Old signature
689	Span create(String spanName, Span parent, SpanContextPropagationExtractor extractor)
690	
691	// New signature
692	Span create(String spanName, String spanKind, Span parent, SpanContextPropagationExtractor
693	extractor)
694	----
695	
696	==== `org.apache.camel.telemetry.SpanDecorator`
697	
698	A new method must be implemented:
699	
700	[source,java]
701	----
702	String getSpanKind(String operation)
703	----
704	
705	This method should return the appropriate SpanKind based on the operation. Most implementations can
706	extend from:
707	
708	- `AbstractSpanDecorator` (returns `INTERNAL` for all operations)
709	- `AbstractHttpSpanDecorator` (returns `CLIENT` for `EVENT_SENT`, `SERVER` for `EVENT_RECEIVED`)
710	- `AbstractMessagingSpanDecorator` (returns `PRODUCER` for `EVENT_SENT`, `CONSUMER` for `EVENT_RECEIVED`)
711	
712	==== Reduced span verbosity for endpoint-sending processors
713	
714	Processors that send to an endpoint (`to`, `toD`, `wireTap`, `enrich`) no longer emit a redundant
715	processor span (`EVENT_PROCESS`) wrapping the endpoint event span (`EVENT_SENT`). Previously these
716	EIPs produced two spans in a parent-child relationship — the processor span added no value because
717	the event span already captures the destination, timing, and status.
718	
719	A new `org.apache.camel.EndpointSending` marker interface in `camel-api` identifies processors that
720	will produce their own endpoint span via `ExchangeSendingEvent`. The telemetry intercept strategy
721	skips creating a processor span for these processors.
722	
723	This reduces span tree depth and total span count for routes that use `to`, `toD`, `wireTap`, or
724	`enrich`. Non-send EIPs (`log`, `bean`, `split`, `aggregate`, etc.) continue to produce processor
725	spans as before.
726	
727	If you have custom telemetry code that relies on the processor span existing as a parent of the
728	endpoint span for `to`, `toD`, `wireTap`, or `enrich`, you will need to adjust your span hierarchy
729	expectations.
730	
731	==== Route-level filtering with `traceCustomIdOnly`
732	
733	The `traceCustomIdOnly` option now also applies at the route level. Routes without an explicit
734	`.routeId()` are excluded entirely from tracing — no route span, no processor spans, and no
735	endpoint send spans are created within them. Previously, `traceCustomIdOnly` only filtered
736	individual processors within all routes.
737	
738	This means that if you relied on `traceCustomIdOnly=true` while leaving routes with auto-generated
739	IDs, those routes will now produce zero spans. To restore tracing for a route, assign it an explicit
740	`.routeId()`.
741	
742	=== camel-opentelemetry2
743	
744	In order to prevent a potential leak when running asynchronous components we need to rethink the implementation details of `camel-opentelemetry2` and remove the  `Scope` wrapping that, when asynchronous, was opening the `Scope` in a thread and closing in another (what we had called "dirty" context). We are now removing this wrapping and moving this part exclusively in the custom Camel `Processors`. Here Camel will take care to open the Opentelemetry scope and close it within the same thread.
745	
746	What it means is that, from now on, the final user or any third party dependency can only "control" the Opentelemetry context within the boundary of a Processor execution.
747	
748	This is just an informative note, there is not action expected by the final user.
749	
750	=== camel-djl
751	
752	The `nlp/word_embedding` application no longer ships a built-in zoo predictor.
753	The only zoo model previously listed for this application — `ai.djl.mxnet:glove:0.0.2` —
754	relied on the discontinued Apache MXNet engine and never loaded successfully (the
755	corresponding integration test had been permanently `@Disabled`). The
756	`ZooWordEmbeddingPredictor` class has been removed and the `nlp/word_embedding`
757	branch of `ModelPredictorProducer.getZooPredictor` now reports `Application not
758	supported` for that path.
759	
760	Routes that use a custom word-embedding model (no `artifactId`, with explicit
761	`model` and `translator` parameters) are unaffected — `CustomWordEmbeddingPredictor`
762	and the `nlp/word_embedding` branch of `getCustomPredictor` remain in place.
763	
764	=== camel-aws-bedrock
765	
766	The `applyGuardrail` producer operation now reads the guardrail identifier from a new dedicated header
767	`CamelAwsBedrockGuardrailIdentifier` (constant `BedrockConstants.GUARDRAIL_IDENTIFIER`, typed `String`)
768	instead of `CamelAwsBedrockGuardrailConfig`. The `CamelAwsBedrockGuardrailConfig` header is typed
769	`GuardrailConfiguration` and is reserved for the `converse` and `converseStream` operations; the
770	previous code path silently produced `null` whenever a route mixed `converse` and `applyGuardrail`
771	calls. If you were not setting the guardrail identifier via header, the endpoint-level
772	`guardrailIdentifier` option continues to work without changes.
773	
774	=== camel-jgroups - potential breaking change
775	
776	The Exchange header constants in `JGroupsConstants` have been renamed to follow
777	the Camel naming convention used across the rest of the component catalog. The
778	Java field names are unchanged; only the header string values have changed:
779	
780	[options="header"]
781	|===
782	| Constant | Previous value | New value
783	| `JGroupsConstants.HEADER_JGROUPS_CHANNEL_ADDRESS` | `JGROUPS_CHANNEL_ADDRESS` | `CamelJGroupsChannelAddress`
784	| `JGroupsConstants.HEADER_JGROUPS_DEST` | `JGROUPS_DEST` | `CamelJGroupsDest`
785	| `JGroupsConstants.HEADER_JGROUPS_SRC` | `JGROUPS_SRC` | `CamelJGroupsSrc`
786	| `JGroupsConstants.HEADER_JGROUPS_ORIGINAL_MESSAGE` | `JGROUPS_ORIGINAL_MESSAGE` | `CamelJGroupsOriginalMessage`
787	|===
788	
789	This is a breaking change for routes that read or write these headers by
790	their literal string value. Routes that reference the constant symbolically
791	(for example `setHeader(JGroupsConstants.HEADER_JGROUPS_DEST, ...)`) continue
792	to work without changes. Routes that set the header by its literal string
793	value (for example `setHeader("JGROUPS_DEST", ...)`) must be updated to use
794	the new value (`setHeader("CamelJGroupsDest", ...)`).
795	
796	=== camel-elasticsearch-rest-client
797	
798	The Exchange header constants in `ElasticSearchRestClientConstant` have been
799	renamed to follow the Camel naming convention used across the rest of the
800	component catalog. The Java field names are unchanged; only the header string
801	values have changed:
802	
803	[options="header"]
804	|===
805	| Constant | Previous value | New value
806	| `ElasticSearchRestClientConstant.ID` | `ID` | `CamelElasticsearchId`
807	| `ElasticSearchRestClientConstant.SEARCH_QUERY` | `SEARCH_QUERY` | `CamelElasticsearchSearchQuery`
808	| `ElasticSearchRestClientConstant.INDEX_SETTINGS` | `INDEX_SETTINGS` | `CamelElasticsearchIndexSettings`
809	| `ElasticSearchRestClientConstant.INDEX_NAME` | `INDEX_NAME` | `CamelElasticsearchIndexName`
810	| `ElasticSearchRestClientConstant.OPERATION` | `OPERATION` | `CamelElasticsearchOperation`
811	|===
812	
813	Routes that reference the constant symbolically (for example
814	`setHeader(ElasticSearchRestClientConstant.SEARCH_QUERY, ...)`) continue to
815	work without changes. Routes that set the header by its literal string value
816	(for example `setHeader("SEARCH_QUERY", ...)`) must be updated to use the
817	new value (`setHeader("CamelElasticsearchSearchQuery", ...)`).
818	
819	=== camel-cxf
820	
821	The Exchange header constants in `CxfConstants` (module `camel-cxf-common`, shared
822	by `camel-cxf` and `camel-cxfrs`) have been renamed to follow the Camel naming
823	convention used across the rest of the component catalog. The Java field names are
824	unchanged; only the header string values have changed:
825	
826	[options="header"]
827	|===
828	| Constant | Previous value | New value
829	| `CxfConstants.OPERATION_NAME` | `operationName` | `CamelCxfOperationName`
830	| `CxfConstants.OPERATION_NAMESPACE` | `operationNamespace` | `CamelCxfOperationNamespace`
831	|===
832	
833	Routes that reference the constant symbolically (for example
834	`setHeader(CxfConstants.OPERATION_NAME, ...)`) continue to work without changes.
835	Routes that set the header by its literal string value (for example
836	`setHeader("operationName", ...)`) must be updated to use the new value
837	(`setHeader("CamelCxfOperationName", ...)`).
838	
839	In particular, the documented `cxfrs` `SimpleConsumer` dispatch idiom that routes
840	on the operation name by its literal header name must be updated:
841	
842	._Java-only: updated CXF operation name header in recipientList_
843	[source,java]
844	----
845	// before
846	from("cxfrs:bean:rsServer?bindingStyle=SimpleConsumer")
847	    .recipientList(simple("direct:${header.operationName}"));
848	
849	// after
850	from("cxfrs:bean:rsServer?bindingStyle=SimpleConsumer")
851	    .recipientList(simple("direct:${header.CamelCxfOperationName}"));
852	----
853	
854	==== Behaviour change: cross-transport propagation of the operation header
855	
856	Because the renamed header value now begins with `Camel`, it is filtered by the
857	standard transport `HeaderFilterStrategy` (`JmsHeaderFilterStrategy`,
858	`HttpHeaderFilterStrategy`, etc.) when crossing a transport boundary, by design
859	— `Camel*` headers are framework-internal and are not propagated over the wire.
860	
861	Routes that bridge an external transport (JMS, HTTP, ...) into a `cxf:` producer
862	and select the SOAP operation from a header supplied by the sender must
863	therefore carry the operation in a non-`Camel`-prefixed application header and
864	map it to `CxfConstants.OPERATION_NAME` (`CamelCxfOperationName`) in the route
865	between the transport `from` and the `cxf:` `to`:
866	
867	[source,xml]
868	----
869	<!-- before (pre-4.21) -->
870	<route>
871	    <from uri="jms:queue:bridge.cxf"/>
872	    <to uri="cxf://bean:serviceEndpoint"/>
873	</route>
874	<!-- caller sets the header keyed by the pre-4.21 value:
875	     setHeader("operationName", "greetMe") -->
876	
877	<!-- after -->
878	<route>
879	    <from uri="jms:queue:bridge.cxf"/>
880	    <setHeader name="CamelCxfOperationName">
881	        <simple>${header.operationName}</simple>
882	    </setHeader>
883	    <to uri="cxf://bean:serviceEndpoint"/>
884	</route>
885	<!-- caller sets a non-Camel-prefixed application carrier header (any name
886	     that is not stripped by the transport HeaderFilterStrategy works);
887	     the route restores the CXF operation header after the transport hop. -->
888	----
889	
890	The same pattern applies to HTTP-based bridges (`platform-http`/`jetty`/`netty
891	-http`/`http` -> `cxf:`) and any other transport whose default
892	`HeaderFilterStrategy` filters `Camel*` headers.
893	
894	==== WS-Security dependency is now optional
895	
896	The `cxf-rt-ws-security` dependency in `camel-cxf-soap` is now **optional**. Most Camel CXF users do
897	not need WS-Security / SAML support, yet the dependency transitively pulled in 13+ `org.opensaml`
898	JARs hosted on a third-party Maven repository (`build.shibboleth.net`) that has proven unreliable,
899	causing intermittent CI and build failures.
900	
901	If your project uses WS-Security features (signatures, encryption, username tokens, SAML), you will
902	need to add `cxf-rt-ws-security` explicitly to your project's POM:
903	
904	[source,xml]
905	----
906	<dependency>
907	    <groupId>org.apache.cxf</groupId>
908	    <artifactId>cxf-rt-ws-security</artifactId>
909	</dependency>
910	----
911	
912	If you also need OpenSAML (for SAML-based WS-Security), add the `org.opensaml` dependencies
913	explicitly and configure the Shibboleth Maven repository (`https://build.shibboleth.net/nexus/content/repositories/releases`)
914	in your project's POM or Maven `settings.xml`.
915	
916	=== camel-dns - potential breaking change
917	
918	The Exchange header constants in `DnsConstants` have been renamed to follow the
919	Camel naming convention used across the rest of the component catalog. The Java
920	field names are unchanged; only the header string values have changed:
